title: Executive Order 8683—Establishing Naval Defensive Sea Areas Around and Naval Airspace Reservations Over the Islands of Rose, Tutuila, and Guam

flags.justification: The Secretary of the Navy is authorized to prescribe regulations and grant entry authorizations — real regulatory discretion, though narrow and standard for a military security-zone designation of this era.
A single named official (Secretary of the Navy) is clearly responsible for enforcement and regulation.
Single coherent purpose: establishing security zones around three named islands.

Penalties are specifically cross-referenced to existing statutes (Criminal Code §44 for sea-area violations, the Civil Aeronautics Act for airspace violations), and the trigger (unauthorized entry) is clearly defined.
No incentive structure rewarding behavior contrary to the order's purpose is identifiable.

No emergency declaration is cited as the basis for this order (it relies on standing Criminal Code and Air Commerce Act authority); a standing military security-zone designation of this kind is evergreen by nature, not an emergency measure requiring periodic reauthorization.
No existing oversight mechanism is removed.
Enforcement explicitly includes 'the cooperation of the local law enforcement officers of the United States' — a real, named enforcement partner beyond self-policing by the Navy alone.
A standard, narrow military security-zone designation, consistent with established practice for the era; no novel precedent identifiable from the text.
The local law enforcement cooperation clause is a coordination request, not a redirection of another agency's mandate.
The exception for U.S. public vessels/aircraft is narrow and does not undermine the security zone's purpose.

notes: Validation batch (positive). Structural weight: 1/22 = 4.5%. A clean, well-defined order.
